title: What Size Kite Do I Need? — Complete Sizing Guide
meta_title: What Size Kite Do I Need? — Weight & Wind Chart
meta_description: What size kite do I need? Complete kite sizing chart by rider weight and wind speed. Expert answer from Surf Store.
meta_keywords: what size kite do i need,kite size calculator,kite size chart
body_html:
What Size Kite Do I Need?

The short answer: your weight and your local wind speed determine your kite size. Heavier riders need bigger kites. Stronger wind needs smaller kites. Here's the chart:

Kite Size Chart (Twin-Tip Riding)

Weight10–15 kts15–20 kts20–25 kts25–30 kts30+ kts
55–65 kg12–14m9–11m7–9m5–7m4–6m
65–80 kg13–15m10–12m8–10m6–8m5–7m
80–95 kg14–17m11–13m9–11m7–9m6–8m
95+ kg15–19m12–15m10–12m8–10m7–9m

How to Read This Chart

These ranges assume a delta/hybrid kite (like Duotone Evo or Cabrinha Switchblade) on a standard twin-tip board. C-kites run about 1m smaller. Foil kites run 2–3m smaller for the same conditions. If you're foil boarding (hydrofoil), you can typically ride 2–3m smaller than twin-tip sizes.

Your First Kite Size

If you're buying your first kite and can only afford one, choose the size that covers your most common wind range. For most European riders at 75–85kg, that's a 10–12m kite covering 15–25 knots. Add a second kite after your first season.

Building a Quiver

  • Two kites: 9m + 12m covers 12–30 knots for 80kg
  • Three kites: 7m + 10m + 13m covers almost everything
  • Foil quiver: 7m + 10m covers 8–25 knots on a foil

FAQ

Q: Can one kite size cover all conditions?
A: No single kite covers everything. But a well-chosen 10–12m (for 80kg) handles the widest wind range — typically 14–28 knots.

Q: Does kite brand affect sizing?
A: Slightly. Some kites generate more power per square metre than others. But the chart above works for all major brands within ±1m.

Q: What if I'm between sizes?
A: Go larger if you ride light-wind spots. Go smaller if you prefer powered, aggressive riding.
excerpt: Complete kite sizing guide with chart by rider weight and wind speed.

title: How Long Does a Wetsuit Last? — Lifespan & Care Guide
meta_title: How Long Does a Wetsuit Last? — Expert Answer
meta_description: How long does a wetsuit last? 2-4 seasons with proper care. Learn the signs of a worn-out wetsuit and how to extend its life.
meta_keywords: how long does wetsuit last,wetsuit lifespan,when to replace wetsuit
body_html:
How Long Does a Wetsuit Last?

A quality wetsuit lasts 2–4 seasons of regular use (40–100 sessions per season). Budget suits may degrade faster; premium suits with careful maintenance can last 5+ seasons. The biggest factors affecting lifespan are: how often you use it, how well you care for it, and the quality of the neoprene.

Signs Your Wetsuit Needs Replacing

  • Seams leaking: If cold water enters consistently through seams that were previously sealed, the GBS tape has failed. Small sections can be repaired; widespread leaking means replacement.
  • Neoprene has lost stretch: Grab the neoprene and pull. Fresh neoprene bounces back immediately. Worn neoprene stays stretched — like a rubber band that's been stretched too many times.
  • Persistent cold despite correct thickness: If your 4/3mm suit no longer keeps you warm in 14°C water, the neoprene cells have compressed and lost insulating capacity.
  • Visible surface damage: Cracking, peeling, or delamination on the outer fabric indicates UV damage and structural degradation.
  • Zippers failing: Stuck, corroded, or broken zippers are repairable but costly. If the zip fails frequently, the suit is near end of life.

How to Make Your Wetsuit Last Longer

  1. Rinse after every use — fresh water removes salt crystals that degrade neoprene and corrode zippers
  2. Dry inside-out in shade — UV destroys neoprene faster than anything. Never dry on a radiator or in direct sun
  3. Store flat or on a wide hanger — thin wire hangers create shoulder creases that crack over time
  4. Don't fold — roll loosely or hang. Fold creases weaken the neoprene at the bend point
  5. Use wetsuit shampoo quarterly — removes salt buildup and body oils that degrade the thermal lining

FAQ

Q: Does wetsuit thickness decrease over time?
A: Yes — neoprene compresses with use. A 4/3mm suit may perform like a 3/2mm after 3 seasons. This is why it feels colder.

Q: Can I repair a leaking wetsuit?
A: Small seam leaks can be fixed with neoprene glue and seam tape (€10–15 kit). Widespread leaking usually means the suit is worn out.

Q: Are expensive wetsuits more durable?
A: Generally yes. Premium suits use higher-grade neoprene and better seam construction. But the main durability factor is care, not price.
excerpt: Wetsuit lifespan guide: expect 2-4 seasons with proper care. Signs of wear and how to extend suit life.

title: What Is Wing Foiling? — The Complete Explanation
meta_title: What Is Wing Foiling? — Beginner Explanation
meta_description: What is wing foiling? A handheld wing + hydrofoil board lets you fly above water using wind. Learn how it works and what gear you need.
meta_keywords: what is wing foiling,wing foil explained,how does wing foiling work
body_html:
What Is Wing Foiling?

Wing foiling is a water sport where you hold an inflatable wing in your hands, stand on a board equipped with a hydrofoil, and use wind power to fly above the water surface. It combines elements of windsurfing (wind power), kitesurfing (board riding), and foiling (flying above water) into a sport that's simpler to set up than any of them.

How Does It Work?

You stand on a buoyant board with a hydrofoil attached underneath. The hydrofoil is an underwater wing that generates lift when moving through water — exactly like an airplane wing generates lift in air. You hold the inflatable wing above your head to catch the wind, which propels the board forward. Once you reach enough speed (about 8–10 knots of board speed), the hydrofoil lifts the board out of the water and you fly silently above the surface.

The sensation is unlike any other water sport — smooth, quiet, and remarkably efficient. With no board dragging through the water, friction almost disappears. You can ride in lighter wind than windsurfing or kitesurfing and cover long distances with minimal effort.

What Gear Do You Need?

  • Wing: An inflatable canopy (4–7m) with rigid handles. You hold it with both hands, switching sides when you change direction. It weighs 2–4kg.
  • Board: A buoyant board (60–130L depending on level) with a foil mount on the bottom. Bigger boards for learning; smaller for advanced riders.
  • Hydrofoil: An aluminium or carbon assembly — mast (60–95cm), front wing (800–2000cm²), fuselage, and stabiliser. The front wing size determines how much lift is generated.
  • Wetsuit + helmet: Standard water sports protection. Helmet is essential because the foil has sharp edges underwater.

Is Wing Foiling Hard to Learn?

Wing foiling has a steeper initial learning curve than SUP but a shallower curve than kitesurfing. Most riders with any board sport experience achieve their first foil flights within 5–15 sessions. The hardest part is coordinating the wing with balance on the foil — once your body learns this, progression is rapid.

Why Is Wing Foiling So Popular?

  • Low wind threshold: Ride in 10–12 knots where other sports can't
  • Minimal setup: Pump wing, attach foil, go. No lines, no rigging, no launch assistance
  • Quiet: No motor, no spray, no noise — just smooth flight
  • Compact gear: Everything fits in a car boot
  • Accessible spots: Works on lakes, rivers, and ocean — anywhere with wind and water

FAQ

Q: How much does wing foil gear cost?
A: A complete beginner setup costs €1,800–€3,500. Used gear can reduce this to €1,000–€2,000.

Q: Do I need lessons?
A: Highly recommended. 2–3 hours with an instructor teaches safety and prevents bad habits that slow progression.

Q: Can children wing foil?
A: Yes — from about age 12 with appropriate gear sizing. Lighter riders need larger wings relative to their weight.
excerpt: Wing foiling explained: how it works, what gear you need, and why it's the fastest-growing water sport in the world.

title: Can You Kitesurf Without Lessons?
meta_title: Can You Kitesurf Without Lessons? — Honest Answer
meta_description: Can you learn to kitesurf without lessons? Technically yes, but it's dangerous and slow. Here's why lessons matter and what they cost.
meta_keywords: kitesurf without lessons,learn kitesurfing alone,do i need kite lessons
body_html:
Can You Kitesurf Without Lessons?

Technically yes. Practically no. Kitesurfing involves a powerful kite that can drag you across the beach, into obstacles, or out to sea if you don't know how to control and depower it. Every year, self-taught riders cause serious injuries — to themselves and bystanders. Professional instruction isn't optional; it's a safety requirement.

Why Lessons Are Essential

Safety systems: Modern kites have multiple depower and quick-release mechanisms. Learning to use them instinctively — before you need them in an emergency — requires hands-on instruction. YouTube videos cannot teach muscle memory.

Wind assessment: Choosing the right kite size, launching safely, and recognising dangerous conditions (offshore wind, gusty thermals, obstacles downwind) requires knowledge that takes instructors years to develop. Beginners don't know what they don't know.

Right-of-way rules: Kitesurfers share water with swimmers, boats, windsurfers, and other kiters. The rules of the water are taught in lessons and are essential for everyone's safety.

Efficiency: A professional instructor gets most students riding upwind in 8–12 hours. Self-teaching the same skill typically takes 50–100+ hours — if you don't quit from frustration first.

What Kite Lessons Cover

  1. Hours 1–3: Kite control on the beach — flying, relaunching, body dragging
  2. Hours 4–6: Water work — body dragging upwind, board recovery, waterstarting
  3. Hours 7–10: First rides — board starts, riding across the wind, stopping
  4. Hours 10–12: Upwind riding — the skill that makes you independent

What Do Lessons Cost?

Group lessons (2–4 students): €200–€400 for 8–10 hours
Private lessons (1-on-1): €400–€800 for 8–10 hours
This is a one-time investment that saves you hundreds of hours and potentially your life.

FAQ

Q: Can I practice with a trainer kite first?
A: Yes — a 2–3m trainer kite (€50–€100) builds basic kite flying skills safely. But you still need real lessons before using a full-size kite.

Q: What if there are no kite schools near me?
A: Travel to a kite destination (Tarifa, Fuerteventura, Egypt) for a learn-to-kite holiday. Many spots offer week-long courses.

Q: Can I take just a few hours of lessons?
A: We recommend minimum 8 hours. Less than that and you likely won't reach the critical skill of upwind riding — meaning you can't ride independently.
excerpt: Honest answer: you shouldn't kitesurf without lessons. Here's why, what lessons cover, and what they cost.

title: How to Choose a SUP Board — Size & Type Guide
meta_title: How to Choose a SUP Board — Complete Guide
meta_description: How to choose a SUP board: inflatable vs hardboard, size chart by weight, and which type suits your paddling style. Surf Store guide.
meta_keywords: how to choose sup board,sup board size guide,isup vs hardboard,best sup for beginners
body_html:
How to Choose a SUP Board

Stand-up paddleboarding is the most accessible water sport — but choosing the wrong board makes it frustrating instead of fun. Board length, width, thickness, and type all affect stability, speed, and how much you'll enjoy your time on the water. Here's how to get it right.

Inflatable vs Hardboard

Inflatable (iSUP): Packs into a backpack. Stores in a closet. Nearly indestructible. Modern drop-stitch iSUPs are remarkably rigid — close to hardboard stiffness at 15 PSI. Best for: 90% of recreational paddlers, anyone with limited storage, families, travel.

Hardboard (composite): Stiffer, faster, and more responsive. Better for surfing, racing, and serious touring. Requires roof racks or a large vehicle for transport. Best for: dedicated paddlers who ride 30+ days/year and have storage space.

Our recommendation: Start with an iSUP. If you paddle 40+ days/year and want to race or surf, add a hardboard later.

Size Chart by Rider Weight

WeightAll-RoundTouringSurf
50–65 kg10'0" × 30"11'6" × 29"8'6" × 29"
65–80 kg10'4" × 32"12'0" × 30"9'0" × 30"
80–95 kg10'8" × 33"12'6" × 31"9'6" × 31"
95+ kg11'0" × 34"13'0" × 32"10'0" × 32"

Board Types

All-round: 10'–10'8", 30"–34" wide. Stable, versatile, good for everything from flat-water cruising to small waves. The right first board for most people.

Touring: 11'6"–14', narrower (28"–31"). Designed for distance with a displacement hull that tracks straight. Faster but less stable than all-round boards.

Surf: 8'–10', shorter and narrower. Designed for catching and riding waves. Not suitable for flat-water cruising.

Yoga/fitness: 10'–11', extra wide (33"–36"). Maximum stability for on-board exercises.

Our Top Picks

Best all-round iSUP: Fanatic Fly Air 10'4" — stable, well-built, includes paddle and pump.
Best touring iSUP: Fanatic Ray Air 12'6" — efficient tracking, premium construction.
Best value: Duotone Pace — solid quality at a competitive price.

FAQ

Q: Do I need a paddle with my SUP?
A: Yes. Most iSUP packages include a 3-piece adjustable paddle. Upgrade to a carbon paddle if you paddle frequently — the weight savings reduce arm fatigue significantly.

Q: Can two people stand on one SUP?
A: On a large board (10'8"+, 33"+), yes — but stability is reduced. Tandem-specific boards exist for this purpose.

Q: How long does an iSUP last?
A: 5–10+ years with proper care. Store deflated in shade, rinse after saltwater, and avoid dragging on rough surfaces.

title: What Wetsuit Thickness Do I Need? — Temperature Chart
meta_title: What Wetsuit Thickness Do I Need? — Temp Guide
meta_description: What wetsuit thickness do I need? Match thickness to water temperature with our chart. Expert sizing from Surf Store.
meta_keywords: wetsuit thickness guide,wetsuit temperature chart,what thickness wetsuit
body_html:
What Wetsuit Thickness Do I Need?

Match thickness to water temperature — not air temperature, not the season, not what your friend wears. Water cools your body 25x faster than air. A sunny 20°C day means nothing if the water is 12°C.

The Definitive Thickness Chart

Water TempThicknessTypeExtras Needed
24°C+1–2mmVest, shorty, or rashguardNone
20–24°C2mmSpring suit or shortyNone
17–20°C3/2mmFull suitOptional boots
14–17°C4/3mmFull suit, sealed seamsBoots recommended
10–14°C5/4mmFull suit, sealed seamsBoots + gloves
7–10°C5/4mm hoodedFull suit + hoodBoots + gloves + hood
Below 7°C6/5mm hoodedFull suit + built-in hoodThick boots + mitts

What Do the Numbers Mean?

A 4/3mm" suit has 4mm neoprene on the torso (where you need warmth) and 3mm on the arms and legs (where you need flexibility). The first number is always the thicker panel. Similarly
hero_image_filename:
4mm limbs.

Common Mistakes

Buying too thin: Most European water is colder than people expect. If in doubt
schema_type: go one step thicker — you can always unzip slightly to cool down
schema_json:
but you can't add warmth to a thin suit.

Ignoring wind chill: Wet neoprene in 20-knot wind feels much colder than the water temperature suggests. Kitesurfers and windsurfers should go one thickness up compared to surfers in the same water.

Trusting air temperature: A 25°C day in spring may have 14°C water. Always check actual water temperature before choosing your suit.

FAQ

Q: Do I need different wetsuits for different sports?
A: The thickness guide applies to all water sports. Sport-specific differences are in cut (arm freedom
related_taxon_permalinks:
knee reinforcement) not thickness.

Q: Is a 4/3mm warm enough for winter in the Mediterranean?
A: Mediterranean winter water is typically 14–16°C. A 4/3mm with boots works for most riders. Below 13°C

consider a 5/4mm.

Q: Can I wear a thicker wetsuit in summer?
A: You can

but you'll overheat. A too-thick suit causes exhaustion and dehydration. Match thickness to water temperature.
